questions.to_find: Which of the given sets of strings are regular languages

understand.plain: We have four languages defined over the alphabet {a}. We need to determine which ones are regular. A language over a unary alphabet {a} is regular if and only if it can be described by a DFA or a regular expression. Non-regular unary languages typically involve non-linear exponents like n^2 or n!.

keywords.explain: A language accepted by a finite automaton (DFA/NFA) or described by a regular expression
A necessary condition for regularity: strings long enough can be 'pumped' (middle part repeated) and still stay in the language
keywords.example: Used to prove {a^(n^2)} is not regular

keywords.explain: An alphabet with a single symbol, e.g., {a}; regularity here depends on whether the set of lengths forms a semilinear set
keywords.example: {a^n | n is even} is regular over {a}

understand.visual_alt: Four candidate languages over {a}: I uses linear exponent (always regular), II uses square (non-regular), III uses product mn (all positive integers, so a+, regular), IV uses factorial (non-regular)

given.aim: Identify which of the four given languages are regular

terms.meaning: Strings a^(2m) for m >= 0 — even-length strings of a (including empty if m=0), i.e., the set of all even powers of a
terms.connects: This is (aa)* which is a regular expression, so I is regular
Gaps between consecutive squares grow without bound; pumping lemma shows this is not regular

terms.meaning: Strings whose length is a product mn for some m,n >= 1; i.e., lengths are all positive integers (since every k >= 1 = 1*k)
terms.example: a, aa, aaa, aaaa, ... — every non-empty string of a's
terms.connects: This equals a^+ = {a^k | k >= 1}, which is regular
Factorial growth is super-polynomial; pumping lemma shows this is not regular

given.plan: For each set, determine if the set of string-lengths forms a semilinear set (which is equivalent to regularity for unary alphabets). Linear sets like even numbers or all positive integers are regular; quadratic or factorial sets are not.

steps.title: Analyze Set I: {a^(2m) | m >= 0}
steps.apply: The exponent 2m produces lengths 0, 2, 4, 6, ... — all even non-negative integers. This is exactly the language described by (aa)*, which is a regular expression. So Set I is REGULAR.
steps.note: Note: the 'n >= 2' in the original statement appears to be a constraint unrelated to the exponent 2m; the key point is the exponent is linear in m.

steps.title: Analyze Set II: {a^(n^2) | n >= 0}
steps.apply: Lengths are 0, 1, 4, 9, 16, 25, ... The difference between consecutive square numbers (n+1)^2 - n^2 = 2n+1 grows without bound. By the pumping lemma: suppose it were regular with pumping length p. Take the string a^(p^2). Pumping it gives a^(p^2 + i*k) for various k. But between p^2 and (p+1)^2 = p^2 + 2p + 1, there are no valid lengths for i >= 1, k > 0 unless 2p+1 > p, contradiction for large p. So Set II is NOT regular.
steps.note: Perfect-square lengths violate regularity because gaps grow linearly.

steps.title: Analyze Set III: {a^(mn) | m >= 1, n >= 1}
steps.apply: For any integer k >= 1, we can write k = 1 * k, so every positive length k is in this set. Thus {a^(mn) | m,n >= 1} = {a^k | k >= 1} = a^+, which is described by the regular expression a^+. So Set III is REGULAR.
steps.note: The set of composite + prime numbers together cover all integers >= 2, and k=1 is covered by m=1, n=1.

steps.title: Analyze Set IV: {a^(n!) | n >= 0}
steps.apply: Lengths are 0! = 1, 1! = 1, 2! = 2, 3! = 6, 4! = 24, ... Factorial growth is super-exponential. The gaps between consecutive factorials grow without bound: (n+1)! - n! = n*n!. By pumping lemma argument similar to Set II, this language is NOT regular.
steps.note: Rapidly growing sparse sets over a unary alphabet are never regular.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.apply: Sets I and III are regular; Sets II and IV are not. Answer: B (I and III only).
steps.note: For unary alphabets, regularity is equivalent to the set of lengths being a semilinear (ultimately periodic) set.

questions.formula_note: Regularity over unary alphabets: a language {a^n | n in S} is regular iff S is a semilinear (ultimately periodic) set of non-negative integers. Pumping Lemma is used to show non-regularity of quadratic and factorial growth sets.

questions.question:
Let P be a regular language and Q be a context-free language such that Q is a subset of P. (For example, let P be the language represented by the regular expression p* and Q be {p^n q^n | n >= 0}.) Which of the following is ALWAYS regular?

A. P ∩ Q
B. P - Q
C. Sigma* - P
D. Sigma* - Q
questions.to_find: Which of the given language operations always produces a regular language

understand.plain: P is a regular language and Q is a context-free language that is a subset of P. We want to find which of the four given language operations is guaranteed to always be regular, regardless of which specific P and Q are chosen (satisfying the given constraints). Regular languages are closed under complement and intersection with regular languages, but not under subtraction with CFLs.

keywords.explain: A class of languages is closed under an operation if applying that operation to language(s) in the class always produces a language still in the class
keywords.example: Regular languages are closed under union, intersection, complement, but NOT under arbitrary subtraction with non-regular languages

keywords.explain: A language generated by a context-free grammar; more powerful than regular languages
keywords.example: {a^n b^n | n >= 0} is CFL but not regular

understand.visual_alt: Venn diagram: Q is a proper subset of P (CFL inside regular). Operations: P∩Q=Q (CFL, not always regular), P-Q (regular minus CFL, not always regular), Sigma*-P (complement of regular = regular), Sigma*-Q (complement of CFL = co-CFL, not always regular)

given.aim: Identify which language operation on P (regular) and Q (CFL, subset of P) always yields a regular language

terms.meaning: A language accepted by a finite automaton
terms.example: P = a*b* (all strings of a's followed by b's)
terms.connects: Regular languages are closed under complement, union, intersection

terms.meaning: A context-free language that is strictly contained in P
terms.example: Q = {a^n b^n | n >= 0} subset P = a*b*
terms.connects: Q may not be regular, so operations involving Q may not preserve regularity

terms.meaning: The set of all strings over alphabet Sigma
terms.example: Sigma* = {a}* = {epsilon, a, aa, aaa, ...} for Sigma={a}
terms.connects: Sigma* is regular, so Sigma*-P is the complement of P, which is also regular

given.plan: Check each option: (A) P∩Q = Q since Q subset P — Q is CFL, not always regular. (B) P-Q: regular minus CFL — not closed, could be non-regular. (C) Sigma*-P: complement of regular P — always regular (regulars are closed under complement). (D) Sigma*-Q: complement of CFL — this is a co-CFL, not always regular.

steps.title: Analyze option A: P ∩ Q
steps.formula_raw: Q subset P => P ∩ Q = Q
steps.apply: Since Q is a subset of P, every string in Q is also in P. Therefore P ∩ Q = Q. But Q is a CFL and may not be regular (e.g., {a^n b^n}). So A is NOT always regular.
steps.note: Intersection of regular and CFL is CFL (not necessarily regular).

steps.title: Analyze option B: P - Q
steps.apply: P - Q removes the CFL Q from the regular language P. Regular languages are NOT closed under subtraction with CFLs. Example: P = a*b*, Q = {a^n b^n | n >= 0}. Then P - Q = {a^i b^j | i != j, i,j >= 0} ∪ {a^i b^j | some other condition} — this is context-free but NOT regular. So B is NOT always regular.
steps.note: P - Q = P ∩ (complement of Q); complement of CFL is co-CFL, not always CFL or regular.

steps.title: Analyze option C: Sigma* - P
steps.apply: P is regular. Regular languages are closed under complement. Therefore Sigma* - P is always regular, regardless of what Q is (Q doesn't even appear in this expression). So C is ALWAYS regular.
steps.note: This is the complement of P, which is a fundamental closure property of regular languages.

steps.title: Analyze option D: Sigma* - Q
steps.apply: Q is a CFL. CFLs are NOT closed under complement. For example, if Q = {a^n b^n c^n | n >= 0} (which is not even CFL, but for some CFLs) — actually complement of some CFLs may not be CFL or regular. Sigma* - Q is a co-CFL, not necessarily regular. So D is NOT always regular.
steps.note: Co-CFLs properly contain CFLs; they are not closed under complement.

questions.formula_note: Regular languages are closed under complement (Sigma* - L is regular if L is regular). They are NOT closed under set difference with CFLs. Key closure properties: Regular closed under union, intersection, complement, concatenation, Kleene star.

questions.question:
Given the language L = {ab, aa, baa}, which of the following strings are in L*?
1. abaabaaabaa
2. aaaabaaaa
3. baaaaabaaaab
4. baaaaabaa

A. 1, 2 and 4
B. 2, 3 and 4
C. 1, 2 and 3
D. 1, 3 and 4
questions.to_find: Which of the given strings belong to L* (Kleene star of L = {ab, aa, baa})

understand.plain: L* is the Kleene star of L, meaning all strings formed by concatenating zero or more strings from L. L = {ab, aa, baa}. We need to check which of the four given strings can be decomposed as a concatenation of one or more strings from {ab, aa, baa}.

keywords.explain: The set of all strings formed by concatenating zero or more strings from L, including the empty string
keywords.example: If L = {a, b}, then L* includes epsilon, a, b, aa, ab, ba, bb, aaa, ...

keywords.explain: Breaking a string into parts, each of which must be an element of L
keywords.example: For L = {ab, aa}, 'abaa' = 'ab'+'aa' is a valid decomposition

understand.visual_alt: L = {ab, aa, baa}. Check each candidate string by trying all possible decompositions into L-words.

given.aim: Check which strings can be written as concatenations of words from L = {ab, aa, baa}

terms.meaning: The base language with three strings of lengths 2, 2, and 3
terms.example: ab has length 2, aa has length 2, baa has length 3
terms.connects: Any string in L* must have a valid left-to-right parsing using these three tokens

terms.example: Try: ab+aa+baa+abaa? No. Try: ab+aa+baa+ab+aa = ab|aa|baa|ab|aa — lengths 2+2+3+2+2=11. Yes!
terms.connects: Valid decomposition found: ab + aa + baa + ab + aa

terms.example: baa+aa+ab+aa? baa+aa+ab+aa = 3+2+2+2=9. Check: b-a-a|a-a|a-b|a-a = baa+aa+ab+aa. Yes!
terms.connects: Valid decomposition: baa + aa + ab + aa = baaaaabaa? Let us verify: baa=baa, aa=aa, ab=ab, aa=aa -> baa+aa+ab+aa = baaaaabaa. Yes!

given.plan: For each string, attempt to parse it left-to-right using strings from L = {ab, aa, baa}. A string is in L* iff it can be fully consumed by a sequence of L-words.

steps.title: Check string 1: abaabaaabaa (length 11)
steps.apply:
Try: ab + aa + baa + ab + aa
= (ab)(aa)(baa)(ab)(aa)
= a,b | a,a | b,a,a | a,b | a,a
Concatenated: a-b-a-a-b-a-a-a-b-a-a = 'abaabaaabaa'. Length = 2+2+3+2+2 = 11. Match! String 1 is in L*.
steps.note: Multiple decompositions may exist; finding one valid one is sufficient.

steps.title: Check string 2: aaaabaaaa (length 9)
steps.apply: Try: aa + aa + baa + aa = (aa)(aa)(baa)(aa) — lengths 2+2+3+2 = 9. Concatenated: a-a-a-a-b-a-a-a-a = 'aaaabaaaa'. Match! String 2 is in L*.
steps.note: Decomposition: aa | aa | baa | aa works perfectly.

steps.title: Check string 3: baaaaabaaaab (length 11)
steps.apply: String starts with 'b'. Only word in L starting with 'b' is 'baa'. So first token must be 'baa'. Remaining: 'aaabaaaab' (length 8). From 'a': options are 'ab' or 'aa'. Try 'aa': remaining 'abaaaab' (6). Try 'ab': remaining 'aaaab' (5). Now 'a': try 'aa': remaining 'aab' (3). Try 'aa' again: remaining 'b' (1) — 'b' alone is not in L. Try 'ab': remaining 'aab' (3) — 'aa'+'b' fails (b alone not in L). Try 'baa' after 'aa': 'aa+baa' = 5 chars consumed from 'aaaab' starting at pos 5 of remaining — let me recheck: after baa+aa+ab = 3+2+2=7, remaining = 'aaab' (4). 'aa'+'ab' = 4 -> baa+aa+ab+aa+ab = 3+2+2+2+2=11. String would be: b-a-a+a-a+a-b+a-a+a-b = 'baaaabaab' -- no that's 10. Systematic: 'baaaaabaaaab' — b is at positions 0,5,9. Only L-word starting with b is 'baa'. So decompositions must use 'baa' at positions starting with b. The string has b at index 0, 5, 9 (0-indexed). After 'baa' (0-2), we have 'aaabaaaab'. Next b is at index 3 of 'aaabaaaab' = index 5+1? Let me recount: 'baaaaabaaaab' = b,a,a,a,a,a,b,a,a,a,a,b (length 12, not 11). Actually length = 12. Now try all: starts with 'baa'(0-2), remaining = 'aaabaaaab'(length 9). From 'a': try 'aa'(0-1), remaining = 'abaaaab'(7). From 'a': try 'ab'(0-1), remaining = 'aaaab'(5). From 'a': try 'aa'(0-1), remaining = 'aab'(3). From 'a': try 'aa'(0-1), remaining = 'b'(1) -- fail. try 'ab' from 'aab': remaining = 'b' -- fail. From 'aab' try 'baa' -- starts with 'a' not 'b'. Dead end. Other branch: from 'aaaab'(5): try 'aa'+'aab' -> 'aab' -> fail as above. try 'ab' -- 'a' then 'b'? 'aaaab' first char 'a', second 'a' -- 'ab' would need index 0='a' index 1='b' but index 1='a'. So 'ab' not possible from 'aaaab' position 0. try 'baa' -- first char 'a' not 'b'. Dead end. So string 3 is NOT in L*.
steps.note: No valid decomposition of 'baaaaabaaaab' into L-words exists; string 3 is not in L*.

steps.title: Check string 4: baaaaabaa (length 9)
steps.apply: Start with 'baa' (only option since starts with 'b'). Remaining: 'aaabaa' (length 6). From 'a': try 'aa', remaining 'abaa' (4). From 'a': try 'ab', remaining 'aa' (2). From 'a': try 'aa', remaining '' (0). Done! Decomposition: baa + aa + ab + aa = 3+2+2+2 = 9. Concatenated: b-a-a-a-a-a-b-a-a = 'baaaaabaa'. Match! String 4 is in L*.
steps.note: Valid decomposition: baa | aa | ab | aa.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.apply: Strings 1, 2, and 4 are in L*. String 3 is not. Answer: A.
steps.note: Always parse greedily left-to-right and backtrack if needed when checking L* membership.

questions.formula_note: Kleene star L* membership: a string w is in L* iff it can be decomposed as w = w_1 w_2 ... w_k where each w_i is in L. Check by left-to-right parsing with backtracking.

questions.question:
Consider the languages L1 = phi (empty set) and L2 = {a}. Which one of the following represents L1* . L2*?

A. {epsilon}
B. {a}*
C. phi
D. L2 is regular.
questions.to_find: The language L1* . L2* where L1 = phi (empty language) and L2 = {a}

understand.plain: L1 is the empty language (no strings at all, not even epsilon). L2 = {a} contains just the single string 'a'. L1* is the Kleene star of the empty language. L2* is a* (all strings of a's). We need to find the concatenation L1* concatenated with L2*. The key insight is that the Kleene star of the empty language is {epsilon}, not phi.

keywords.explain: The language with NO strings at all — not even the empty string epsilon
phi* = {epsilon} because Kleene star is defined as L^0 ∪ L^1 ∪ L^2 ∪ ... and L^0 = {epsilon} for any L
keywords.example: phi* = phi^0 = {epsilon} (since phi^k = phi for k>=1, only phi^0 contributes)

keywords.explain: L1.L2 = {xy | x in L1, y in L2}; if L1 = phi, then L1.L2 = phi
keywords.example: phi . {a}* = phi (no first part to concatenate)

understand.visual_alt: phi* = {epsilon}, {a}* = {epsilon, a, aa, aaa, ...}. Concatenation {epsilon}.{a}* = {a}* ... wait, let me recalculate.

given.aim: Compute L1* . L2* for L1 = phi, L2 = {a}

terms.meaning: The empty language containing no strings
{xy | x in L1*, y in L2*}

given.plan: Compute L1* = phi* = {epsilon} (since phi^0 = {epsilon} and phi^k = phi for k>=1). Compute L2* = {a}* = {epsilon, a, aa, ...}. Concatenate: L1*.L2* = {epsilon}.{a}* = {a}* (since {epsilon} is the identity for concatenation).

steps.title: Compute L1* = phi*
steps.apply: L1 = phi (empty language). phi^0 = {epsilon} by definition (zero concatenations of any language always gives {epsilon}). phi^k = phi for all k >= 1 (concatenating phi with anything gives phi). Therefore phi* = {epsilon} ∪ phi ∪ phi ∪ ... = {epsilon}.
steps.note: The Kleene star of the empty language is {epsilon}, NOT phi.

steps.title: Compute L2* = {a}*
steps.apply: {a}^0 = {epsilon}, {a}^1 = {a}, {a}^2 = {aa}, ... So {a}* = {a^n | n >= 0} = a* (the regular language of all strings of a's, including epsilon).
steps.note: This is the standard Kleene star of a single-character language.

steps.title: Compute L1* . L2* = {epsilon} . {a}*
steps.formula_raw: {epsilon} . L = L for any language L
steps.apply:
L1*.L2* = {epsilon} . {a}* = {xy | x in {epsilon}, y in {a}*} = {epsilon.y | y in {a}*} = {y | y in {a}*} = {a}*.
So L1*.L2* = {a}* = {epsilon, a, aa, aaa, ...}.
steps.note: {epsilon} is the identity element for concatenation.

steps.title: Match with answer options
steps.apply: Result is {a}*, which matches option B. Option A = {epsilon} would be the answer only if the operation were L1* alone or L1.L2*. Since L1*.L2* = {epsilon}.{a}* = {a}*, the answer is B.
steps.note: Key distinction: phi* = {epsilon} (not phi), and {epsilon}.L = L.

questions.formula_note: phi* = {epsilon} (Kleene star of empty language is {epsilon}). Concatenation identity: {epsilon}.L = L. Key: {a}* = {a^n | n >= 0} = all strings of a's including epsilon.

questions.question:
Which one of the following is TRUE?

A. The language L = {a^n | n is prime} is regular.
B. The language L = {(ab)^n | has at least as many occurrences of (110)s as (011)s} is regular.
C. The language L = {w | w has at least as many occurrences of (110)s as (011)s} is regular.
D. The language L = {ww^R | w in {0,1}*, with Sigma = {0,1}} is regular.
questions.answer: C. The language L = {w | w has at least as many occurrences of (110)s as (011)s} is regular.
questions.to_find: Which of the given languages is regular

understand.plain: We need to check four languages and determine which one is regular. The image (GATE 2014 Set 1) shows option C as the correct answer: the language of all binary strings where the count of substring '110' is >= count of substring '011'. This is a subtle regularity result — it turns out counting '110' vs '011' occurrences can be tracked by a finite automaton. The other options (prime lengths, palindromes ww^R) are classic non-regular languages.

keywords.explain: Tracking how many times a given pattern appears as a contiguous substring of a string
w^R is the reverse of w; ww^R is a string followed by its reverse, which is always a palindrome
A formal notation for describing regular languages using union, concatenation, and Kleene star

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='520' height='200' viewBox='0 0 520 200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='0' y='0' width='520' height='200' rx='12' class='bg'/><text x='260' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>GATE 2014: Checking Regularity</text><rect x='10' y='50' width='240' height='60' rx='8' class='no' opacity='0.3'/><text x='130' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>A: {a^n | n prime}</text><text x='130' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>NOT regular (pumping lemma)</text><rect x='270' y='50' width='240' height='60' rx='8' class='ok' opacity='0.3'/><text x='390' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>C: count(110) >= count(011)</text><text x='390' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>REGULAR (finite automaton exists)</text><rect x='10' y='130' width='240' height='50' rx='8' class='no' opacity='0.3'/><text x='130' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>D: ww^R (palindromes)</text><text x='130' y='170' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>NOT regular (pumping lemma)</text><rect x='270' y='130' width='240' height='50' rx='8' class='no' opacity='0.3'/><text x='390' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>B: (ab)^n count comparison</text><text x='390' y='170' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>NOT always regular</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Grid showing four options: A (prime lengths, not regular), B (not regular), C (count 110 >= count 011, regular), D (palindromes, not regular). C is the only regular language.

given.aim: Identify the one regular language among the four given options

terms.term: Option A: L = {a^n | n is prime}
terms.meaning: Strings of a's whose length is a prime number
terms.connects: Primes are not ultimately periodic, so this is not regular (proved by pumping lemma)

terms.term: Option C: L = {w | w has at least as many 110s as 011s}, Sigma = {0,1}
terms.example: '1101' has one '110' and zero '011', so it's in L
terms.connects: Key insight: #(110) - #(011) can be related to a finite quantity trackable by DFA states

terms.meaning: Even-length palindromes: string concatenated with its reverse
terms.connects: Not regular — requires remembering arbitrary-length prefix w, impossible with finite state

given.plan: Eliminate non-regular options using pumping lemma or closure properties, then verify the remaining option is regular.

steps.title: Eliminate Option A: {a^n | n is prime}
steps.apply: Let p be the pumping length. Take string a^q where q is a prime > p. Write it as xyz with |xy| <= p and |y| = k >= 1. Then xy^i z = a^(q + (i-1)k). For i = q+1, length = q + q*k = q(1+k), which is composite (divisible by q and by 1+k both > 1 for q > 1). This contradicts the pumping lemma. So A is NOT regular.
steps.note: Primes form a non-semilinear set over unary alphabet.

steps.title: Eliminate Option D: {ww^R | w in {0,1}*}
steps.apply: Take string 0^n 1^n 1^n 0^n (which is ww^R for w = 0^n 1^n). For pumping length p, pump within the first 0^n block: xy^i z would give 0^(n+k) ... 0^n which is no longer of the form ww^R. So D is NOT regular.
steps.note: {ww^R} requires matching the first half with the reverse of second half — inherently needs unbounded memory.

steps.title: Verify Option C: L = {w | #(110) >= #(011)} is regular
steps.apply: Key observation: consider any string w. Note that #(110) and #(011) are related by the structure of the string. A crucial insight is that #(110) - #(011) depends only on the suffix/prefix structure: specifically, each '1' that transitions from '0...0' region affects the balance. It can be shown that for any string w, |#(110) - #(011)| <= 1, because '110' and '011' overlap in structure ('1' is the end of '011' and start of '110'). Therefore the difference is always in {-1, 0, +1}, making it trackable by a finite automaton with a constant number of states. A DFA can be constructed to track the running difference, which stays bounded.
steps.note: The key insight: #(110) - #(011) is always in {-1, 0, 1} for any binary string, because every new '110' occurrence also contributes to potential '011' occurrences and vice versa in an overlapping manner. This bounded difference is trackable by finite state.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.apply: Options A, B, D are not regular. Option C is regular because the difference #(110) - #(011) remains bounded (in {-1, 0, 1}), allowing a DFA to recognize when it is >= 0. Answer: C.
steps.note: This is a classic GATE question testing the ability to identify when a counting condition can be handled with finite state.

solution.result: C. The language L = {w | w has at least as many occurrences of (110)s as (011)s} is regular.

questions.formula_note: Pumping Lemma for non-regularity proofs. Key insight for option C: the count difference #(110) - #(011) is always bounded in {-1, 0, 1} for any binary string, making it recognizable by a finite automaton.
